question_text: Let $( X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , X _ { 3 } )$ be a trivariate normal random vector with mean vector $( - 3 , 1 , 4 )$ and variance-covariance matrix $\left[ { \begin{array} { c c c } { 4 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 3 } & { - 3 } \\ { 0 } & { - 3 } & { 4 } \end{array} } \right]$ . Which of the following statements are true? (i) $X _ { 2 }$ and $X _ { 3 }$ are independent (ii) $X _ { 1 } + X _ { 3 }$ and $X _ { 2 }$ are independent. (iii) $( X _ { 2 } , X _ { 3 } )$ and $X _ { 1 }$ are independent. (iv) ${ \scriptstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } ( X _ { 2 } + X _ { 3 } )$ and $X _ { 1 }$ are independent. (A) (i) and (iii) (B) (ii) and (iii) (C) (i) and (iv) (D) (iii) and (iv)
solution:
To determine which of the given statements are true, we need to analyze the properties of the trivariate normal distribution and the relationships between the random variables \(X_1\), \(X_2\), and \(X_3\).

Given:
- Mean vector: \(\mu = (-3, 1, 4)\)
- Variance-covariance matrix: \(\Sigma = \left[ \begin{array}{ccc} 4 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 & -3 \\ 0 & -3 & 4 \end{array} \right]\)

### Step 1: Check if \(X_2\) and \(X_3\) are independent
Two random variables \(X_2\) and \(X_3\) are independent if their covariance is zero. From the variance-covariance matrix, we see that:
\[
\text{Cov}(X_2, X_3) = -3
\]
Since \(\text{Cov}(X_2, X_3) \neq 0\), \(X_2\) and \(X_3\) are not independent. Therefore, statement (i) is false.

### Step 2: Check if \(X_1 + X_3\) and \(X_2\) are independent
Two random variables \(Y = X_1 + X_3\) and \(X_2\) are independent if their covariance is zero. First, we find the covariance between \(Y\) and \(X_2\):
\[
\text{Cov}(Y, X_2) = \text{Cov}(X_1 + X_3, X_2) = \text{Cov}(X_1, X_2) + \text{Cov}(X_3, X_2)
\]
From the variance-covariance matrix, we have:
\[
\text{Cov}(X_1, X_2) = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \text{Cov}(X_3, X_2) = -3
\]
Thus:
\[
\text{Cov}(Y, X_2) = 0 + (-3) = -3
\]
Since \(\text{Cov}(Y, X_2) \neq 0\), \(X_1 + X_3\) and \(X_2\) are not independent. Therefore, statement (ii) is false.

### Step 3: Check if \((X_2, X_3)\) and \(X_1\) are independent
Two random vectors \((X_2, X_3)\) and \(X_1\) are independent if their cross-covariance matrix is the zero matrix. The cross-covariance matrix between \((X_2, X_3)\) and \(X_1\) is:
\[
\text{Cov}((X_2, X_3), X_1) = \left[ \begin{array}{cc} \text{Cov}(X_2, X_1) & \text{Cov}(X_3, X_1) \end{array} \right]
\]
